paragraphs.text:
Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

- PERSISTENT HOMOLOGY CAPTURES THE GENERAL - IZATION OF NEURAL NETWORKS WITHOUT A VALI DATION SET

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

**----- Start of picture text -----**

**----- End of picture text -----**

Figure 1: Our proposal.

ABSTRACT

The training of neural networks is usually monitored with a validation (holdout) set to estimate the generalization of the model. This is done instead of measuring intrinsic properties of the model to determine whether it is learning appropriately. In this work, we suggest studying the training of neural networks with Algebraic Topology, specifically Persistent Homology (PH). Using simplicial complex representations of neural networks, we study the PH diagram distance evolution on the neural network learning process with different architectures and several datasets. Results show that the PH diagram distance between consecutive neural network states correlates with the validation accuracy, implying that the generalization error of a neural network could be intrinsically estimated without any holdout set.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Generalization is what makes a machine learning model useful; the uncertainty of its behaviour with unseen data is what makes it potentially dangerous. Thus, understanding the generalization error of a model can be considered one of the holy grails of the entire machine learning field.

Machine learning practitioners typically monitor some metrics of the model to estimate its generalization error and stop the training even before the numerical convergence to prevent the overfitting of the model. Usually, the error measure or the metric relevant to the task is computed for a holdout set, the validation set. Since these data have not been directly used for updating the parameters, it is assumed that the performance of the model on the validation set can be used as a proxy of the generalization error, provided it is representative of the data that will be used in inference. One can, though, potentially overfit to this holdout set if is repeatedly used for guiding a hyperparameter search.

Instead of relying on an external set, though, the question of whether it could be possible to estimate the generalization error with some intrinsic property of the model is highly relevant, and it has been

1

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

barely explored in the literature. On the other hand, Algebraic Topology has recently been gaining momentum as a mathematical tool for studying graphs, machine learning algorithms, and data.

In this work, we have the goal of, once having characterized neural networks as weighted, acyclic graphs, represented as Algebraic Topology objects (following previous works), computing distances between consecutive neural network states. More specifically, we can calculate the Persistent Homology (PH) diagram distances between a give state (i.e., when having a specific weights during the training process) and the next one (i.e., after having updated the weights in a training step), as depicted in Figure 1. We observe that during the training procedure of neural networks we can measure this distance in each learning step, and show that there exists a high correlation with the corresponding validation accuracy of the model. We do so in a diverse set of deep learning benchmarks and model hyperparameters. This shines light on the question of whether the generalization error could be estimated from intrinsic properties of the model, and opens the path towards a better theoretical understanding of the dynamics of the training of neural networks.

In summary, our contributions are as follows:

- Based on principles of Algebraic Topology, we propose measuring the distances (Silhouette and Heat) between the PH persistence diagrams obtained from a given state of a neural network during the training procedure and the one in the immediately previous weights update.

- We empirically show that the evolution of these measures during training correlate with the accuracy in the validation set. We do so in diverse benchmarks (MNIST, CIFAR10, CIFAR100, Reuters text classification), and models (MLPs in MNIST and Reuters, MLPs and CNNs in CIFAR100 and CIFAR100).

- We thus provide empirical proof of the fact that valuable information related to the learning process of neural networks can be obtained from PH distances between persistence diagrams (we will call this process _homological convergence_ ). In particular, we show that homological convergence is related to learning process and the generalization properties of neural networks.
- In practice, we provide a new tool for monitoring the training of neural networks, and open the path to estimating their generalization error without a validation set.

The remainder of this article is as follows. In Section 2 we describe the theoretical background of our proposal in terms of Algebraic Topology, while in Section 3 we go through the related work. Then, in Section 4 we formalize our method. Finally, in sections 6 and 7 we present and discuss our empirical results, respectively.

## 2 BACKGROUND

In this section we introduce the mathematical foundations of this paper. A detailed mathematical description is included in the Supplementary Material.

A simplicial complex is a set composed of points, line segments, triangles, and their n-dimensional counterparts, named simplex ( _K_ ). In particular, a simplicial complex must comply with two properties: 1. Every face of a simplex is also in the simplicial complex (of lower dimension). 2. The non-empty intersection of any two simplices contained on a simplicial complex is a face of both. 0,1,2,3-simplex and non simplex examples are shown in Figure 2.

**----- Start of picture text -----**

(a) 0,1,2,3-simplex
(b) Non-simplex
**----- End of picture text -----**

Figure 2: Simplex and non-simplex examples.

We can associate to an undirected graph, _G_ = ( _V, E_ ), a simplicial complex where all the vertices of G are the 0-simplex of the simplicial complex and the complete subgraphs with _i_ vertices, in _G_

2

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

**----- Start of picture text -----**

𝝏 𝝏 𝝏
3-simplex 2-simplex 1-simplex 0-simplex
**----- End of picture text -----**

Figure 4: Boundary function sample.

corresponds to a ( _i −_ 1)-simplex. This type of construction is usually called a complex clique on the graph G, and is denoted by _Cl_ ( _G_ ). Figure 3 shows a graph clique complex Cl(G) example.
The boundary function is defined as a map, from an _i_ -simplex to an ( _i −_ 1)-simplex, as the sum of its ( _i −_ 1)-dimensional faces. A boundary function sample is shown in Figure 4.

In algebraic topology, a _k_ -chain is a combination of _k_ -simplices (sometimes symbolized as a linear combination of simplices that compose the chain). The boundary of a _k_ -chain is a ( _k −_ 1)chain. It is the linear and signed combination of chain element boundary simplices. The space of _i_ -chains is denoted by _Ci_ ( _K_ ).

Figure 3: Graph clique complex Cl(G) example.

There are two special cases of chains that will be useful to define homology group:

- Closed chain or _i_ -cycle: _i_ -chain with empty boundary. An _i_ -chain _c_ is an _i_ -cycle if and only if _∂ic_ = 0, i.e. _c ∈ ker_ ( _∂i_ ). This subspace of _Ci_ ( _K_ ) is denoted as _Zi_ ( _K_ ).

- Exact chain or _i_ -boundary: An _i_ -chain _c_ is an _i_ -boundary if there exists an ( _i_ + 1)-chain _d_ such that _c_ = _∂i_ +1( _d_ ), i.e. _c ∈ im_ ( _∂ i_ + 1). This subspace of _Ci_ ( _K_ ), the set of all such i-boundaries forms, is denoted by _Bi_ ( _K_ ).

Now, if we consider _i_ -cycles not bounding an ( _i_ + 1)-simplicial complex, this is the definition of an _i_ -th homology group of the simplicial complex _K_ . The precise definition is the quotient group of _Bi_ ( _K_ ) module _Zi_ ( _K_ ) (i.e. _Bi_ ( _K_ ) _/Zi_ ( _K_ ), see Supplementary Material). The number of non equivalent _i_ -cycles (Figure 5) is the dimension of the homology group _Hi_ ( _K_ ), also named Betti numbers.

Figure 5: The two blue dashed cycles are homologically equivalent, the pink isn’t.

We can create a nested family of simplicial complexes, _Kε_ , where at each step _t_ , _Kεt_ is embedded in the simplicial complex _Kεt_ +1 . We call this set a simplicial complex filtration.

For each filtration simplicial complex, we can calculate the homology groups. Then, we can look at the birth, that is, when a homology class appears, and death, the time when the homology class disappears. The PH treats the birth and the death of these homological features in _Kε_ for different _ε_ values. The lifespan of each homological feature can be represented as an interval ( _birth, death_ ), of the homological class. Given a filtration, this collection of intervals is named a Persistence Diagram (PD) Carlsson (2009).

It is possible to compare two PDs using specific

distances (Wasserstein and Bottleneck). To efficiently perform this operation, due to the size of these diagrams, it is sometimes necessary to simplify them by means of a discretization process (such as Weighted Silhouette and Heat vectorizations).

3

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## 3 RELATED WORK

**Algebraic Topology and Machine Learning** The use of Algebraic Topology in the fields of data science and machine learning has been gaining momentum in recent years (see Carlsson (2009)). Specifically in the case of neural networks, some works have applied topology for improving the training procedure of the models Hofer et al. (2020); Clough et al. (2020), or pruning the model afterwards Watanabe & Yamana (2020b). Other works have focused on analyzing the capacity of neural networks Guss & Salakhutdinov (2018a); Rieck et al. (2019b); Konuk & Smith (2019) or the complexity of input data Konuk & Smith (2019). Furthermore, recent works have provided topological analysis of the decision boundaries of classifiers based on PH and Betti numbers Ramamurthy et al. (2019); Naitzat et al. (2020).

**Graph and topological representations of neural networks** Gebhart et al. (2019) suggest a method for computing the PH over the graphical activation neural networks, while Watanabe & Yamana (2020a) propose representing neural networks via simplicial complexes based on Taylor decomposition, from which one can compute the PH. Chowdhury et al. (2019) show that directed homology can be used to represent MLPs. Anonymous (2021) concurrently show neural networks, when represented as directed, acyclic graphs, can be associated to an Algebraic Topology object, specifically to a directed flag complex. By computing the PH diagram, one can effectively characterize neural networks, and even compute distances between two given neural networks, which can be used to measure their similarity. This is unlike other works Corneanu et al. (2019); Guss & Salakhutdinov (2018b) approximating neural networks representations with regard to the input space. Relevant to our work, in Rieck et al. (2019b) authors propose a complexity measure of neural networks based on persistent homology. However, we will see that their representation does not fulfill our requirements in Section 4.

**Estimating the generalization and studying the learning process** We are, though, specifically interested in the use of PH for analyzing the learning process, especially with the goal of estimating generalization. In this regard, the literature is perhaps more limited. Jiang et al. (2019) work on understanding what drives generalization in deep networks from a Bayesian of view. Neyshabur et al. (2017) study the generalization gap prediction from the training data and network parameters using a margin distribution, which are the distances of training points to the decision boundary. In Li et al. (2020), authors propose an alternative to cross-validation for model selection based on training once on the whole train set, without any data split, deriving a validation set with data augmentation.

Corneanu et al. (2020) try to estimate the performance gap between training and testing using PH measures. They claim. However, one can observe some caveats. The first one is that their regression fitted to predict the test error has a considerably high error, making it not usable in practice. The second caveat is that for fitting the regression one needs at least part of the sequestered testing set.

In this work, motivated by the interest of having a better understanding of whether it would be possible to estimate the generalization of neural networks without a holdout set, we suggest using the topological characterization and distances concurrently proposed in Anonymous (2021) but, crucially, measured between consecutive weight updates. We will show that the evolution of this distance is similar to the one of the validation accuracy. Unlike Li et al. (2020), we do not use any data at all. Unlike Corneanu et al. (2020), we do not build a statistical or machine learning model (linear regression) for predicting the testing error. Instead, we propose a new measure, and we empirically show that it highly correlates with the validation accuracy. Note that in this work we do not work with any input data and activations, but with the parameters of the neural network themselves. The code and outputs are fully available in the Supplementary Material under a MIT License.

## 4 APPROACH

**Representation** For representing neural networks as graphs, we follow the approach proposed concurrently in Anonymous (2021). We associate to the neural network, at each learning state (defined by its weights), a weighted directed graph that is analyzed as an abstract simplicial complex. It is important to note that abstract simplicial complex are used in opposition to geometric simplicial complex (e.g. Vietoris-Rips complex).

4

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

For every training state, neural network connections are considered as directed and weighted edges between neurons, represented by graph nodes. Biases are considered as new edges that join to isolate vertices. In this representation, activation functions are lost. Bias information could also have been ignored because, as we will see, it is not very informative in terms of homology, but we decided to preserve it.

Negative edge weights are represented with reverse edges with the same weight absolute value. We discard the use of the absolute value of weights as neural networks are not invariant to weight sign transformations. This representation is consistent with the fact that every neuron can be replaced by a neuron from which two edges with opposite weights emerge and converge again on another neuron with opposite weights. From an homological point of view, this would be represented as a closed cycle. Weights are normalized following the Equation 1. _ζ_ is an smoothing parameter that we set to 1e-6. This smoothing parameter is necessary as we want to avoid normalized weights of edges to be 0 (in our representation 0 implies a lack of connection):

Unlike Rieck et al. (2019b) we do not represent the neural network as a multipartite graph with a persistent homology filtration that contains at most 1-simplices (edges), which only capturrd zero-dimensional topological information, i.e. connectivity information. We do not believe the strong assumption that the NNs encode the learned information layer pairwise exclusively since there are trivial global transformations of a NN that are not captured by analyzing pairs of layers, more specifically 1. Superfluous cycle insertions: for instance, add two neurons and connect their input to a single neuron in a lower layer and their two outputs to a single output neuron in an upper layer with opposite weights; 2. Identity layer insertions: insert an intermediate identity layer with neurons and trivially connect to the next layer; and 3. Non-planar neural networks analysis: the analysis of neural networks that use multiple connections between non-consecutive layers require higher order topological analysis.

**Algebraic Topology object** For each weighted directed graph associated with the state of a neural network, we link a directed flag complex to it. The topological properties of this directed flag complex are studied using homology groups _Hn_ . We calculate the homology groups up to degree 3 ( _H_ 0- _H_ 3).

For each state, we use a family of simplicial complexes, _Kε_ , for a range of values of _ε ∈_ R. The simplicial complex at step _εt_ is embedded in the complex at _εt_ +1, for _εt ≤ εt_ +1, i.e. _Kε ⊆ Kεt_ +1. _ε_ is used as a filter that establish the minimum weight of the graph representation edges included on the simplicial complex. This collection of contained simplicial complex (associated to a directed weighted graph), called filtration, _Kεmin ⊆ ... ⊆ Kεt ⊆ Kεt_ +1 _⊆ ... ⊆ Kεmax_ , where _t ∈_ [0 _,_ 1] and _εmin_ = 0, _εmax_ = 1 (remember that edge weights are normalized).

The sequence of homology groups is calculated by varying the _ε_ parameter to obtain the persistence homology diagram. In our case, persistent homology calculations are performed on Z2. In other words, once the corresponding filter has been applied to the weight of the edges, all connected edges are considered equally.

**Distances between persistence diagrams of consecutive states** In this paper, we are interested in comparing PDs between different simplicial complex associated to each training state of the neural network. There are two distances traditionally used to compare PDs, Bottleneck distance (the length of the longest edge) and Wasserstein distance (using the sum of all edges lengths, instead of the maximum). Their stability with respect to perturbations on PDs has been object of different studies Chazal et al. (2012); Cohen-Steiner et al. (2005). As shown in comparative studies such as in **?** , different distances and different ways of vectorizing persistence diagrams have results with different levels of stability and quality.

In order to make computations feasible and obviate noisy intervals, we filter the PDs by limiting the minimum PD interval size. We do so by setting a minimum threshold _η_ = 0 _._ 01. Intervals with a lifespan under this value are not considered (spurious homological features). Additionally, for computing distances, we need to remove infinity values. As we are only interested in the deaths until the maximum weight value, we replace all the infinity values by 1 _._ 0.

5

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

In our case, neural networks have millions of persistence intervals per PD, while Wasserstein distance calculations are computationally hard for large PDs. To make it computationally feasible, we will use a vectorized version of PDs, also called PD discretization. This vectorized version summaries have been proposed and used on recent literature Adams et al. (2017); Berry et al. (2020); Bubenik (2015); Lawson et al. (2019); Rieck et al. (2019a). For persistence diagram distance calculation, we use weighted Silhouette and Heat vectorizations, using the Giotto-TDA library Tauzin et al. (2020).

## 5 EXPERIMENTS

**Data** We validate our method in several heterogeneous (vision, natural language), well-known datasets, namely 1. MNIST LeCun & Cortes (2010), 2. CIFAR-10, 3. CIFAR-100 Krizhevsky (2009), and 4. the Reuters dataset Thoma (2017) (multi-class and multi-label document classification dataset).

**Models** We experiment with two neural architectures,1. MLPs and 2. CNNs. In the latter case, we use the convolutional layers as a pre-trained model with frozen weights, and we learn an MLP on top of it. The reason we do so is that our method is based in a representation that, at least in the basic form, does not allow capturing information from convolutional layers. Thus, we need a single (same weights) feature extractor, to abstract away distances related to the CNN layers and focus on the MLP.

**Conducted experiments** We define the _base_ MLP architecture as {Input, Linear(512), Dropout(0.2), Linear(512), Dropout(0.2), Output}. In the case of CNNs, the pre-trained model is defined as 3 convolutional blocks with kernel size 3 (starting with 32 channels), interleaved with max pooling (its linear layers are thrown away after the pre-training). On top of the pre-trained CNN, we also define the same base MLP architecture.

Then, for each dataset and model (MLP and CNN), we experiment with varying (while keeping the rest fixed to the base architecture) 1. Layer size (number of units per layer): 4, 16, 32, 128, 256; 2. Number of labels (the other classes are removed): 2, 4, 6, 8, 10; 3. Learning rate: 1e-e05, 0.0001, 0.001, 0.01, 0.1; 4. Dropout: 0.0, 0.2, 0.4, 0.5, 0.8; and 5. Input order: 5 random reordering of the input samples. As a control experiment, for each analyzed problem we run the same configuration with 5 different input orders. If the measured distances are, indeed, related with the learning process of neural networks, these variations should not have any noticeable effect. We run each configuration 5 times with different random seeds (and, thus, weight initializations[1] ) to see if the results are consistent across runs. All models are trained with the RMSProp optimizer with a batch size of 256.

**Distances and validation accuracy computation** Note that homological distances are obtained at the end of each batch, while validation metrics are only computed on each epoch. We consider the validation curve to be our baseline, and therefore we compare our method to it. The methodology is summarized as follows:
1. In each training step (i.e., batch) we extract the weights from the MLP current state and use them to build an abstract simplicial complex from the associated weighted directed graph.

2. We calculate the homological persistence diagram of the simplicial complex.

3. We then calculate the distance between consecutive persistence diagrams (we will call this sequence _homological convergence_ ). We use two different distances, Heat and Silhouette.
4. We compare the homological convergence with the evolution of the validation results on neural network learning process.

All experiments were executed in a machine with 2 NVIDIA V100 of 32GB, 2 Intel(R) Xeon(R) Platinum 8176 CPU @ 2.10GHz, and of 1.5TB RAM, for a total of around 7 days. We note that our method is considerably demanding in terms of both compute and memory.

## 6 RESULTS

In this section, we highlight the main results, omitting Silhouette (since the results were clearer with Heat). See the Supplementary Material for all the plots and correlations, including Silhouette.

> 1The pre-trained convolutional weights are always identical, though.

6

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

We study the relation between the evolution of the PH diagram distances with the one of the validation score with the cumulative values of the distance between homologous persistence diagrams because this value seems much more stable. The information of the distance between the persistence diagrams has been normalized to visualize clearly the type of evolution of each curve on the same scale. Some of the non-normalized plots can be found in the Supplementary Material. Figure 6 shows the cumulative and non-cumulative homology the MNIST experiment with layer size.

Distance difference (line) Validation score (dashed) Distance difference (line) Validation score (dashed)
**----- End of picture text -----**

Figure 6: Heat distance and validation accuracy curves on MNIST with layer size. Normalized.

For each experiment (e.g., layer size in MNIST), we plot both the evolution of the PH diagram distance and the validation score (accuracy). The plotted values are the corresponding means of the 5 repetitions with different seeds. In addition, we compute the Pearson correlation for these values. Plots show on the x-axis each training step (for each batch) of the evolution in the training state of the neural network. On the y-axis, two scales are shown that apply to the distance curves between accumulated persistence diagrams (solid lines), scale on the right side, and the neural network validation (dotted lines), numerical scale on the left side. For each sub-experiment (for example, different values of layer size) a different color was used.

The general result is that the evolution of the homological convergence of the MLPs seems to be very similar to the one of the validation score. This is generally consistent across experiments (see the Supplementary Material). Table 1 shows the mean (and standard deviations) of the Pearson correlations for all datasets. All means are above 0.8, implying that there is strong correlation. Intuitively, this is also observed in the plots, although once the distances are normalized it is not as clear to visualize. Interestingly, we find that the very few exceptions in which the correlation is low corresponds to extreme values (very small number of neurons per layer, very high learning rate, very high dropout), in which the neural network doesn’t end up learning properly.

In CNNs, the correlations are lower (but still usually above 0.8 in experiments such as the one of increasing the number of layers). Recall that we froze a convolutional feature extractor since our method only supports MLPs. We believe these lower correlations occur because an important part of the learning process happened in the convolutional layers (in the pre-training), not captured.

Another finding is that the method obtains consistent results across runs, meaning that it is capturing information related to important properties of the networks themselves instead of random artifacts. When varying the studied hyperparameters, we observe that the curves for each configuration are indeed, different. Remarkably, in the control experiments, this is not the case; results show that the homological convergence during the learning of the same problem with the same model but with different input order is very similar. The alteration of the order of the input doesn’t have any effect in the homological convergence. The results of two of these experiments are shown in Figure 7.

In addition, we observe that when the neural network learns the given problem, homological convergence occurs. For example, when the layer size is modified, the capacity of the neural network to learn the problem changes (Figure 6). When it can’t learn the problem, because the network does not have sufficient capacity (the layer size is too small, 4 units), the homology does not seem to converge.

Regarding the learning rate, the results are coherent with the intuition that it is a fundamental parameter that controls how much to change the model in response to the estimated error during the learning process. A too small learning rate may result in a long training process that could be stalled, while a too large value may fall in a fast suboptimal solution or an unstable training process. Using homological convergence we find similar behaviour, as can be seen in Figure 9.

Finally, we note that even if the two convergences (validation and homological convergence) are correlated, they are not the same process. This is especially visible in the learning rate experiments. For instance, in Figure 9, homological convergence is reached before the stabilization of the validation accuracy. Presumably, they are not capturing the exact same information; we believe that the difference is due to the fact that the validation accuracy depends on the specifics of the data sampled in the validation subset, while the homological convergence is independent of the validation data.

## 7 DISCUSSION

We posed the question whether homological convergence (in terms of distances between PH diagrams in consecutive neural network states) is related to the learning process of neural networks. We have seen that, indeed, it is the case, with strong empirical results backing our claim. This finding has a remarkable implication. If homological convergence evolution mirrors the validation accuracy curve, one could ignore the validation set to monitor the training. This opens the path towards estimating the generalization of neural networks without the need of any holdout set. Researchers have wondered for a long time whether generalization could be predicted from intrinsic properties of the model or training data alone, and in fact other works have claimed to do so. Although we do not provide any predictive model, we show that our proposed measures strongly correlate with validation accuracy. We do so by not using any data at all; we just look at the neural network itself. Our contribution aims pushing towards a better understanding of the learning process of neural networks, not targeting any specific direct application. However, we note that it can be effectively used for monitoring the training of neural networks in terms of convergence expected generalization, as we have extensively

Figure 9: Learning evolution when modifying the learning rate parameter. Not normalized.

shown in the experiments. Apart from when there is no access to a validation set, this is relevant because depending on a validation set has the risk of overfitting to it. Having an intrinsic measure should be more robust to random noise in a specific data sample.

The main limitation of our method is its computational scalability. This lack of scalability has prevented us from validating our method on bigger models and datasets. However, we note that our approach computes the _exact_ PD distances, that is, we do not simplify the graph representation of the neural networks (we keep every single neuron and connections) and we do not approximate any computation. This leaves room for finding efficient approximations, opening a new research line.

Finally, we note that instead of computing correlations, serving as a basic quantitative study, it would be interesting to perform a time-series analysis to gain more insights on how the two curves vary together. Moreover, it would have been interesting to investigate how to build a predictive model of the validation accuracy from the PH distances, but it is was of the scope of this work.

## 8 CONCLUSIONS & FUTURE WORK

In this work, we have provided an empirical proof of the fact that homological convergence is related to the learning process and generalization properties of neural networks. Furthermore, we have shown that it can be used to monitor the training of a neural network (and potentially estimating its generalization) without a validation set. As future work, we suggest generalizing our representation to other neural architectures and scaling up the experiments to larger models and datasets, for which finding efficient approximations of our method will be crucial.

9

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## REFERENCES

zero_shot__gemini-3-flash-preview.label: Zero Shot (gemini-3-flash-preview)
zero_shot__gemini-3-flash-preview.overall_feedback: The paper investigates a novel and ambitious goal: monitoring neural network generalization using persistent homology (PH) distances between consecutive training states, thereby bypassing the need for a validation set. The empirical results across multiple datasets show a compelling correlation between these topological distances and validation accuracy. However, there are significant technical concerns regarding the formalization of the simplicial complex construction—specifically how directed edges and negative weights are handled—and some potential inconsistencies in the reported correlation results that require clarification.

comments.title: Inconsistent handling of negative weights
comments.quote: Negative edge weights are represented with reverse edges with the same weight absolute value... This representation is consistent with the fact that every neuron can be replaced by a neuron from which two edges with opposite weights emerge and converge again
comments.explanation: The authors state they discard the absolute value of weights to preserve sign information by using 'reverse edges'. However, in the subsequent section on 'Algebraic Topology object', they define the filtration parameter $\epsilon$ as the 'minimum weight' of edges included, and state 'edge weights are normalized' to the range [0, 1]. If negative weights are handled by reversing the edge direction, the network is no longer a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) as claimed in the abstract and Section 3, because any negative weight in a standard feed-forward layer would create a cycle back to a previous layer. Furthermore, if weights are normalized to [0, 1] after this 'reversal', it is unclear how the original sign is actually distinguished by the homology calculation, which the authors say is performed over $Z_2$.

comments.title: Ambiguity in complex construction for directed graphs
comments.quote: We associate to an undirected graph, $G = (V, E)$, a simplicial complex... this construction is usually called a complex clique on the graph G... [In Section 4] we link a directed flag complex to it.
comments.explanation: Section 2 defines the clique complex for *undirected* graphs, while Section 4 claims to use a *directed flag complex*. These are mathematically distinct. A clique complex on an undirected graph defines a $k$-simplex for every $k+1$ clique. A directed flag complex (often called a directed clique complex) requires specific ordering/transitivity in the tournament of the sub-graph. Given that standard MLPs are multipartite, they cannot contain cliques of size $>2$ (triangles) in the undirected sense. The authors claim to compute homology up to $H_3$, which requires at least 4-cliques. It is non-trivial how a standard MLP (even with non-consecutive connections) forms enough higher-dimensional simplices to yield meaningful $H_1, H_2, H_3$ features without the 'superfluous cycle insertions' they criticize in earlier work.

comments.title: Inconsistency in reported correlation values
comments.quote: Table 1: Correlation (c. with 20 points) of validation values with topological difference (cumulative).
comments.explanation: The text states 'All means [of Pearson correlations] are above 0.8, implying that there is strong correlation.' However, looking at Table 1, the value for 'Reuters' is 0.6220, 'CIFAR-10 CNN' is 0.4241, and 'CIFAR-100 CNN' is 0.6130. These values are significantly below the 0.8 threshold mentioned in the prose. While the authors offer a prose justification for the CNN performance related to frozen layers, the statement that 'All means are above 0.8' is factually contradicted by their own table.

comments.title: Mathematical notation error in weight normalization
comments.quote: Weights are normalized following the Equation 1. $\zeta$ is an smoothing parameter that we set to 1e-6. [Equation 1 is missing, but later text implies normalization to [0,1]]
comments.explanation: There is a reference to 'Equation 1' in the 'Representation' paragraph of Section 4, but the actual equation is missing from the text (likely an OCR or layout error in the provided snippet). While I can infer the intent (normalization), the lack of the formal definition makes it difficult to verify how the smoothing parameter $\zeta$ interacts with the filtration $\epsilon$. If $\zeta$ is used to ensure no weight is 0, it directly affects the 'birth' of 0-simplices in the filtration.

comments.title: Clarity of 'Homological Convergence' definition
comments.quote: We then calculate the distance between consecutive persistence diagrams (we will call this sequence *homological convergence*)
comments.explanation: The term 'homological convergence' is used to describe the sequence of distances between PDs at step $N$ and $N-1$. Usually, 'convergence' implies a limit approaching a value (e.g., the distance going to zero). While Figure 6 shows the distance decreasing, defining the *sequence* itself as 'convergence' is slightly imprecise and could mislead a reader into thinking the homology groups themselves are converging to a specific manifold, rather than just the *change* in homology slowing down.

zero_shot__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Zero Shot (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
zero_shot__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback: The paper presents a compelling and innovative application of persistent homology to monitor neural network training without relying on a validation set. The core idea—using topological distances between consecutive network states as a proxy for generalization—is both original and empirically well-supported across multiple datasets and architectures. The authors provide strong empirical evidence of high correlation between homological convergence and validation accuracy, particularly in MLPs. However, several technical concerns arise around methodological specification and consistency, especially regarding the treatment of directed graphs in flag complex construction, normalization procedures, and computational feasibility. While the results are promising, some claims about generalization estimation may slightly overreach the current empirical support, particularly for CNNs where correlations are weaker.

comments.title: Directed flag complex construction underspecified
comments.quote: For each state, we use a family of simplicial complexes, _Kε_ , for a range of values of _ε ∈_ R. The simplicial complex at step _εt_ is embedded in the complex at _εt_ +1, for _εt ≤ εt_ +1, i.e. _Kε ⊆ Kεt_ +1. _ε_ is used as a filter that establish the minimum weight of the graph representation edges included on the simplicial complex.
comments.explanation: The paper claims to follow Anonymous (2021) in using a directed flag complex on a weighted directed graph, but the actual filtration process described appears to rely solely on edge weights as thresholds—this is typical of undirected filtrations like Vietoris-Rips, not directed flag complexes. In directed flag complexes, simplices are formed by transitive paths of directed edges, and filtration typically proceeds on edge existence or directionality, not on edge weights. Using weight magnitude as the filtration parameter ε likely results in a non-standard construction. I checked whether this is resolved by context: the Supplementary Material is not provided, and the reference to Anonymous (2021) only establishes the graph representation, not the filtration scheme. This raises concerns about whether the resulting persistence diagrams truly capture directed topology rather than just connectivity patterns. Without clarification on how directionality influences simplex formation under this filtration, the methodological reproduction is not feasible.

comments.title: Incorrect notation in weight normalization equation
comments.quote: Weights are normalized following the Equation 1. _ζ_ is an smoothing parameter that we set to 1e-6. This smoothing parameter is necessary as we want to avoid normalized weights of edges to be 0 (in our representation 0 implies a lack of connection):
comments.explanation: The paper refers to 'Equation 1' but no equation is visible in the provided text—likely an OCR or formatting issue. However, the surrounding text implies a normalization scheme to prevent zero weights. Standard weight normalization in PH applications typically involves scaling to [0,1] or z-scoring. The mention of ζ = 1e-6 suggests a small constant added to avoid division by zero or log(0), but the exact formula is missing. This omission affects reproducibility. I considered if forward references resolve it: later in the text, weights are normalized with εmin=0, εmax=1, suggesting affine normalization. But without seeing the actual formula, it's ambiguous whether ζ is used additively or as a regularizer. This underspecifies a critical preprocessing step.

comments.title: Inconsistency in reporting Silhouette and Heat results
comments.quote: We highlight the main results, omitting Silhouette (since the results were clearer with Heat). See the Supplementary Material for all the plots and correlations, including Silhouette. ... Table 1: Correlation (c. with 20 points) of validation values with topological difference (cumulative). ||Heat|distance|Silhouette distance|Silhouette distance||
comments.explanation: The text states that results are omitted for Silhouette because Heat gave clearer results, yet Table 1 lists identical values for both Heat and Silhouette distances across all datasets. This is suspicious: it's highly unlikely that two distinct vectorizations produce identical correlations across six different experiments. I checked whether this might be due to identical computation—no, Heat and Silhouette are different vectorizations (one based on Gaussian-weighted integrals, the other on cumulative lifetimes). Either this is a typo in the table (e.g., Silhouette columns duplicated from Heat), or the authors computed only one distance but reported it twice. This undermines confidence in the numerical results. Even if the trend holds, reporting identical correlations for different methods is misleading.

comments.title: Mismatch between text and PH diagram interpretation
comments.quote: We filter the PDs by limiting the minimum PD interval size. We do so by setting a minimum threshold _η_ = 0._01. Intervals with a lifespan under this value are not considered (spurious homological features). Additionally, for computing distances, we need to remove infinity values. As we are only interested in the deaths until the maximum weight value, we replace all the infinity values by 1._0.
comments.explanation: Setting a minimum lifespan threshold η = 0.01 is reasonable. However, the replacement of infinite persistence values with 1.0 is problematic. In standard PH, infinite bars represent features that persist beyond the maximum filtration value. Replacing them with 1.0—the maximum ε value—distorts their geometric meaning: they are no longer 'infinite' but 'maximum'. This especially matters in Wasserstein or vectorized distances, which integrate over (birth, death) intervals: replacing ∞ with 1.0 shrinks the death time to the upper bound, potentially inflating the persistence of short-lived features. The authors justify this by reference to practicality, but this modification can introduce systematic bias in distance calculations, especially if the number of infinite components varies between states. This undermines the interpretability of the distance metric.

comments.title: Overstatement of generalization estimation capability
comments.quote: This shines light on the question of whether the generalization error could be estimated from intrinsic properties of the model... In practice, we provide a new tool for monitoring the training of neural networks, and open the path to estimating their generalization error without a validation set.
comments.explanation: The authors show correlation between homological convergence and validation accuracy, which is strong and promising. However, estimating generalization error requires predicting the test loss, not correlating it asynchronously with an internal metric. The paper never attempts to quantitatively predict the validation accuracy from the PH distance—only to show correlation in trends. While homological convergence may be a useful monitoring tool, calling it a path to 'estimating generalization error' overreaches. Moreover, for CNNs, correlations drop as low as 0.42 (Table 1), undermining broad claims. The authors do acknowledge in limitations that they don’t build a predictive model, but the abstract and conclusion leave a stronger impression of capability than the results currently support.

comments.title: Ambiguity in control experiment interpretation
comments.quote: Results show that the homological convergence during the learning of the same problem with the same model but with different input order is very similar. The alteration of the order of the input doesn’t have any effect in the homological convergence.
comments.explanation: The paper presents this as evidence that the measure captures learning dynamics rather than noise. However, it's unclear whether 'different input order' means per-epoch shuffling or a fixed but different seed. If the input order is reshuffled every epoch, the dynamics could still be similar due to the same batch statistics. The claim would be stronger if the order were fixed (e.g., dataset presented sequentially) and still yielded similar convergence—this would rule out that the method is sensitive only to macro-level training structure. As written, a careful reader might incorrectly conclude that homological convergence is invariant to data ordering in a way that overstates its robustness.

zero_shot__glm-4.6.label: Zero Shot (glm-4.6)
zero_shot__glm-4.6.overall_feedback: The paper presents an interesting approach to monitor neural network training using persistent homology distances between consecutive states, potentially eliminating the need for validation sets. The core idea is novel and the empirical results showing high correlations with validation accuracy across multiple datasets are compelling. However, the paper suffers from several critical issues: a fundamental error in the homology group definition, missing crucial details about weight normalization and handling of negative weights, and insufficient justification for some methodological choices. While the experimental setup is thorough, the statistical analysis of correlations lacks rigor, and some reported results appear inconsistent. The computational scalability concern is acknowledged but not quantified. These issues undermine the reproducibility and theoretical soundness of the approach, though the core contribution remains promising.

comments.title: Incorrect homology group definition
comments.quote: The precise definition is the quotient group of Bi(K) module Zi(K) (i.e. Bi(K) /Zi(K), see Supplementary Material).
comments.explanation: I initially thought this might be a notation choice, but checking standard algebraic topology references (Hatcher, 2002) confirms that the k-th homology group is defined as H_k = Z_k / B_k (cycles modulo boundaries), not B_k / Z_k as stated. The quotient B_k / Z_k would typically be trivial since boundaries are a subgroup of cycles. This error appears to be a fundamental mistake that affects the theoretical foundation of their homology computations. The authors likely meant Z_k / B_k but wrote it incorrectly.

comments.title: Missing weight normalization equation
comments.quote: Weights are normalized following the Equation 1. ζ is an smoothing parameter that we set to 1e-6.
comments.explanation: The paper references Equation 1 for weight normalization but never shows it. This is critical because normalization affects the edge weights used in the simplicial complex construction and filtration. Without knowing the normalization scheme, readers cannot reproduce the method. The smoothing parameter ζ = 1e-6 is mentioned but its role in preventing zero weights needs context in the actual formula.

comments.title: Ambiguous handling of negative weights
comments.quote: Negative edge weights are represented with reverse edges with the same weight absolute value.
comments.explanation: While the authors explain they don't use absolute values to preserve sign information, the description of how negative weights are handled is unclear. 'Reverse edges' could mean either: (1) edges with opposite direction in the directed graph, or (2) a topological construction that preserves orientation information. This ambiguity affects how the simplicial complex is built and could significantly impact the computed homology. The paper should clarify the exact construction.

comments.title: Insufficient filtration construction details
comments.quote: ε is used as a filter that establish the minimum weight of the graph representation edges included on the simplicial complex.
comments.explanation: For directed graphs with potentially negative weights (handled via 'reverse edges'), the paper doesn't explain how this creates a proper filtration (nested sequence of complexes). Standard filtrations typically use absolute values or monotone functions. With their handling of negative weights and ε filtering, it's unclear how K_ε ⊆ K_{ε'} for ε < ε' is ensured. This needs clarification for the persistent homology computation to be valid.

comments.title: Unjustified homology degree limit
comments.quote: We calculate the homology groups up to degree 3 (H0- H3).
comments.explanation: The paper doesn't justify why computing homology only up to dimension 3 is sufficient for their neural networks. Given that modern networks can have complex connectivity patterns, higher-dimensional homology might capture important topological features. While computational constraints are a valid reason, the authors should discuss this limitation or provide evidence that H3 captures all relevant information.

comments.title: Identical correlation values in Table 1
comments.quote:
||Heat|distance|Silhouette distance|Silhouette distance|
|---|---|---|---|---|
|Dataset|Means mean|Deviations mean|Means mean|Deviations mean|
|MNIST|0.8910|0.0424|0.8910|0.0424|
comments.explanation: Table 1 shows identical mean correlation values and standard deviations for Heat and Silhouette distances across all datasets. This is suspicious given that Section 6 states 'omitting Silhouette (since the results were clearer with Heat)'. If Heat gave clearer results, we'd expect some difference in correlations. This could be a copy-paste error in the table or suggest the two methods are producing identical results, which would be noteworthy but unexplained.

comments.title: Lack of statistical significance testing
comments.quote: All means are above 0.8, implying that there is strong correlation.
comments.explanation: The paper reports Pearson correlations but doesn't provide p-values or confidence intervals. With only 20 points per correlation (as noted in Table 1), the statistical significance should be assessed. A correlation of 0.8 with n=20 has a p-value of approximately 0.00001, which is indeed significant, but this should be explicitly stated. More importantly, the paper doesn't address multiple comparison corrections across the many experiments performed.

zero_shot__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.label: Zero Shot (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
zero_shot__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback: The paper presents a novel approach to monitoring neural network training using persistent homology (PH) distances between consecutive weight states. The authors provide a clear conceptual framework and a substantial empirical investigation across multiple architectures and datasets. While the paper makes a compelling case for 'homological convergence' as a proxy for generalization, it suffers from a lack of formal mathematical rigor in the definition of the graph-to-simplicial-complex mapping, specifically regarding the normalization formula and the handling of directed acyclic graphs. Furthermore, while the empirical results are presented as highly correlated, the methodology for this correlation—particularly the transition from raw batch-wise distances to cumulative values for comparison with epoch-wise validation accuracy—is underspecified and requires more justification to rule out cherry-picking.

comments.title: Missing normalization formula
comments.quote: Weights are normalized following the Equation 1. $\zeta$ is an smoothing parameter that we set to 1e-6.
comments.explanation: The text explicitly references 'Equation 1', but no such equation is provided in the provided text. A reader cannot verify or reproduce the normalization procedure without this formula, which is critical given the subsequent claim that the normalization process is necessary to avoid zero-weight edges. This is a moderate issue because the exact handling of weight scaling significantly impacts the resulting PH diagrams.

comments.title: Unclear mapping of DAG to Flag Complex
comments.quote: For every training state, neural network connections are considered as directed and weighted edges between neurons, represented by graph nodes ... For each weighted directed graph associated with the state of a neural network, we link a directed flag complex to it.
comments.explanation: The authors define neural networks as DAGs but then define persistent homology over a 'directed flag complex'. In algebraic topology, the standard construction of a flag complex is on an undirected graph (clique complex). It is not clear how the authors define a 'directed flag complex' (is it a directed path complex? a directed clique complex?) or how the weights of the directed edges are lifted to the higher-dimensional simplices in the filtration. This is a moderate issue regarding methodological reproducibility.

comments.title: Ambiguous relationship between 'Cumulative' and 'Non-cumulative' plots
comments.quote: We study the relation between the evolution of the PH diagram distances with the one of the validation score with the cumulative values of the distance between homologous persistence diagrams because this value seems much more stable.
comments.explanation: The authors state that valid training monitoring is achieved using cumulative distances, but Figure 6 shows both cumulative and non-cumulative plots without clearly explaining the functional form of the accumulation (e.g., is it a running sum, a moving average, or a cumulative sum of differences?). Since the correlation results depend on this transformation, the lack of a formal definition of 'cumulative values' in this context is an ambiguity that makes it difficult to assess whether the correlation is a natural property of the training process or an artifact of the smoothing/accumulation function used.

comments.title: Correlation terminology mismatch
comments.quote: Table 1 shows the mean (and standard deviations) of the Pearson correlations for all datasets. All means are above 0.8, implying that there is strong correlation.
comments.explanation: The authors perform comparisons between batch-wise PH distances and epoch-wise validation accuracy. The text does not specify how they align these two distinct temporal granularities. If they simply downsample the batch-wise data or upsample the epoch-wise data, the correlation calculation could be heavily biased by the choice of interpolation. Given the importance of the claim that this method can estimate generalization, the lack of detail here is concerning.

zero_shot__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Zero Shot (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
zero_shot__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: The paper presents an intriguing application of persistent homology to monitor neural network training by tracking topological distances between consecutive weight states. The core idea of using intrinsic topological evolution as a proxy for generalization is compelling, and the empirical evaluation across multiple datasets and architectures is thorough. However, the manuscript contains several critical issues that must be addressed before publication. Most notably, a fundamental definition in algebraic topology is reversed, the proposed weight normalization formula is missing, and there is a direct contradiction between the text's claim that all correlation means exceed 0.8 and the actual values reported in Table 1. Additionally, the alignment of batch-level topological distances with epoch-level validation metrics is ambiguous, and the phrasing around 'homological convergence' and generalization error estimation could mislead readers about what has actually been demonstrated. These are largely fixable with careful revisions, clarification of methodology, and more precise statistical framing.

comments.title: Reversed homology group definition
comments.quote: The precise definition is the quotient group of Bi(K) module Zi(K) (i.e. Bi(K) /Zi(K))
comments.explanation: I initially wondered if the notation was non-standard or if I was misreading the inclusion relation. Checking the background section, the authors correctly define cycles Zi as kernels of the boundary map and boundaries Bi as images of the next boundary map. In standard algebraic topology, boundaries are a subspace of cycles (Bi ⊆ Zi), so the homology group is defined as cycles modulo boundaries: Hi(K) = Zi(K) / Bi(K). The text states the opposite quotient (Bi / Zi), which is mathematically incorrect given the established inclusion. This is a fundamental error in the background that should be corrected to align with standard conventions.

comments.title: Missing weight normalization formula (Equation 1)
comments.quote: Weights are normalized following the Equation 1. ζ is an smoothing parameter that we set to 1e-6.
comments.explanation: The authors explicitly reference Equation 1 for normalizing edge weights and mention a smoothing parameter ζ, but the equation itself does not appear in the text. Without the formula, it is impossible to determine whether normalization is done per-layer, globally, via softmax, L2 normalization, or min-max scaling. This omission critically hinders reproducibility, as the choice of normalization directly affects the filtration thresholds and persistence diagram computation. I checked the supplementary materials reference and the surrounding text, but the formula is entirely absent.

comments.title: Filtration direction contradicts minimum-weight threshold definition
comments.quote: ε is used as a filter that establish the minimum weight of the graph representation edges included on the simplicial complex. ... Kεt is embedded in the simplicial complex Kεt +1, for εt ≤ εt +1, i.e. Kε ⊆ Kεt +1.
comments.explanation: The authors define ε as a minimum weight threshold, meaning an edge is included only if its weight w ≥ ε. As ε increases, fewer edges satisfy this condition, so the simplicial complex should shrink. However, the authors claim the filtration is nested as Kε ⊆ Kεt+1, implying the complex grows as ε increases. This is a direct logical contradiction. Either ε is actually a maximum weight threshold (where higher thresholds include weaker connections, which is atypical), the inclusion direction is written backwards, or the filtration construction uses descending weight order without explicitly stating it. Clarification is needed to ensure the PH construction is correctly described.

comments.title: Numerical contradiction between text claims and Table 1
comments.quote: Table 1 shows the mean (and standard deviations) of the Pearson correlations for all datasets. All means are above 0.8, implying that there is strong correlation.
comments.explanation: The text explicitly states that all reported correlation means are above 0.8. However, Table 1 clearly lists several values below this threshold: Reuters (0.6220), CIFAR-10 CNN (0.4241), and CIFAR-100 CNN (0.6130). This overstates the consistency of the method. The authors do acknowledge later in Section 6 that CNN correlations are lower due to frozen conv layers, but the initial sweeping claim contradicts their own data. I verified the table values and confirmed the discrepancy. The text should accurately reflect the full range of results or the threshold claim should be removed.

comments.title: Ambiguous temporal alignment for correlation calculation
comments.quote: Note that homological distances are obtained at the end of each batch, while validation metrics are only computed on each epoch.
comments.explanation: The method computes a topological distance after every training batch, but validation accuracy is only available at epoch boundaries. To compute Pearson correlation, these two time series must be aligned, yet the procedure is not described. The table footnote '(c. with 20 points)' suggests they subsampled to the number of epochs, but it is unclear whether they used only the distance at the final batch of each epoch, averaged batch distances per epoch, or interpolated. This lack of detail leaves room for ambiguity in how the correlation was computed and whether temporal smoothing or alignment choices influenced the reported values. Explicitly stating the aggregation or downsampling strategy is necessary for reproducibility.

comments.title: Framing of 'generalization error' and 'homological convergence'
comments.quote: Results show that the PH diagram distance between consecutive neural network states correlates with the validation accuracy, implying that the generalization error of a neural network could be intrinsically estimated without any holdout set. ... we will call this process homological convergence.
comments.explanation: I initially appreciated the pragmatic focus on correlation rather than overclaiming predictive power. However, the authors conflate correlation with validation accuracy and the estimation of generalization error. While accuracy and error are related, they are not identical, and correlation with one does not automatically calibrate the other without a mapping function. Additionally, coining the sequence of distances a 'process of convergence' is terminologically misleading; convergence implies approaching a fixed point or limit, whereas the paper merely describes the trajectory of distances. While the authors note in the discussion that this is not the exact same process and future work could build predictive models, the abstract and title overstate the current evidence. Tightening the language to accurately reflect that the method monitors training dynamics via correlated intrinsic signals would be more precise.

zero_shot__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Zero Shot (deepseek-v4-flash)
zero_shot__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback: The paper proposes an intriguing approach to monitor neural network training using persistent homology distances between consecutive weight states, claiming strong correlation with validation accuracy. However, the manuscript contains several significant issues: a mathematical error in the definition of homology groups, a false claim about correlation magnitudes that contradicts the reported table, suspicious duplication in the correlation table, and a potentially flawed methodological choice to use cumulative sums that could induce spurious correlations. These issues undermine the core empirical claims and need careful revision.

comments.title: Incorrect definition of homology group
comments.quote: The precise definition is the quotient group of B_i(K) module Z_i(K) (i.e. B_i(K)/Z_i(K)).
comments.explanation: The standard definition of the i-th homology group is Z_i(K)/B_i(K) (cycles modulo boundaries), not B_i(K)/Z_i(K). The paper reverses the roles, which is a basic conceptual error. While the surrounding prose correctly mentions cycles and boundaries, this explicit formula is wrong and could confuse readers.

comments.title: Missing normalization equation
comments.quote: Weights are normalized following the Equation 1. ζ is an smoothing parameter that we set to 1e-6.
comments.explanation: Equation 1 is referenced but not shown in the text (possibly omitted during OCR). Without the explicit formula, the normalization procedure is underspecified, making reproduction difficult. The description only mentions a smoothing parameter to avoid zero weights, but the exact transformation is unclear.

comments.title: False claim about correlation means
comments.quote: All means are above 0.8, implying that there is strong correlation.
comments.explanation: Table 1 shows that for Reuters the mean is 0.6220, for CIFAR-10 CNN it is 0.4241, and for CIFAR-100 CNN it is 0.6130. All these are below 0.8, directly contradicting the statement in the results section. This misrepresents the findings and overstates the strength of the correlation.

comments.title: Suspicious duplication in correlation table
comments.quote: Table 1 shows the mean (and standard deviations) of the Pearson correlations for all datasets. ... Heat distance ... Silhouette distance
comments.explanation: The table lists identical mean and standard deviation values for Heat and Silhouette distances across every dataset (e.g., both 0.8910 for MNIST). This is highly unlikely given that they are different vectorizations. The column header structure is also garbled ("Means mean", "Deviations mean", duplicate "Silhouette distance"), indicating a likely formatting error in the table. This needs correction.

comments.title: Potential spurious correlation from cumulative sums
comments.quote: We study the relation between the evolution of the PH diagram distances with the one of the validation score with the cumulative values of the distance between homologous persistence diagrams because this value seems much more stable.
comments.explanation: Cumulative sums of PH distances are monotonically increasing, and validation accuracy tends to also increase during training. Correlating two monotonic series can produce high correlation even if there is no meaningful relationship, due to shared trend. The paper does not detrend or first-difference the series to address this. This methodological flaw threatens the validity of the main empirical claim.

comments.title: Underspecified alignment of batch-level distances with epoch-level validation
comments.quote: In each training step (i.e., batch) we extract the weights from the MLP current state ... We then calculate the distance between consecutive persistence diagrams ... We compare the homological convergence with the evolution of the validation results.
comments.explanation: The PH distance is computed per batch, but validation accuracy is computed per epoch. The paper does not explain how these are aligned to compute correlation (e.g., averaging per epoch, cumulative sum per epoch, or some other aggregation). The plots show epoch on the x-axis, but the exact mapping is not specified, making the analysis difficult to reproduce.

zero_shot__glm-4.7-flash.label: Zero Shot (glm-4.7-flash)
zero_shot__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback: This paper explores the application of persistent homology (PH) to analyze neural network training dynamics, proposing that distances between homological states correlate with generalization (validation accuracy). While the motivation is sound and the empirical results show promising correlations across various architectures and datasets, the paper contains critical omissions that hinder reproducibility. Specifically, the normalization rule for weights referenced as Equation 1 is missing, making the proposed method impossible to reproduce. Additionally, the authors overstate a finding regarding input order invariance, claiming 'no effect' where 'very similar' is the actual evidence. Minor notational issues in the description of the Wasserstein distance and inconsistent axis labeling in figures further distract from the technical contributions.
